content:
Thanks for bringing this up! This is a great example of how the wording of a question can influence the way we answer.

The question "Do you only go out to bars on weekends?" is a yes/no question that contains the word "only," which restricts the time frame to weekends. When someone answers "No" to this question, they are rejecting the idea that they limit their bar visits to weekends.

So if you say "No," it means "I do not only go to bars on weekends," which implies you go on other days too. For example, "No, I go to bars every day" fits perfectly because it clarifies that your bar visits are not confined to weekends.

In contrast, if you answered "Yes," that would mean you do restrict your bar outings to weekends only.

This kind of question is called a negative polarity question or a question with a restrictive adverb ("only"). The answer "No" does not mean "No, I don't go to bars on weekends," but rather "No, I don't only go on weekends."

This phenomenon can sometimes cause confusion because the "No" answer negates the restriction implied by "only."

If you'd like, I can share some more examples or explain this in terms of logic or linguistics. Would that be helpful?
